attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old Indigenous woman presents with jaundice, abdominal pain, weight loss and fevers. She has noticed these symptoms worsening over the last six weeks. Her medical history includes chronic hepatitis B infection for which she is on tenofovir, but has poor compliance with her follow-up arrangements. She has no other medical conditions and takes no medications. She has never smoked or consumed alcohol. Her blood pressure is 125/70 mmHg, pulse 72/min and temperature 37.0˚C. Apart from the jaundice, the physical examination is unremarkable. Her liver function tests are shown:

  • Bilirubin: 173 umol/L (4-20)

  • GGT: 274 U/L (5-50)

  • ALP: 271 U/L (35-110)

  • ALT: 38 U/L (5-40)

  • AST: 96 U/L (10-40)

  • LDH: 334 U/L (120-25)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
The above stem presents a worrying picture for this patient. Her history of jaundice and weight loss are very concerning for cancer, in particular in the hepatobiliary system.

The key in this stem is her history of chronic hepatitis B infection. In Australia, chronic infection with hepatitis B or C is the most common cause of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), an annual risk of 2-5% for patients with cirrhosis due to these infections. The presentation of HCC can be variable, from asymptomatic and picked up on screening ultrasounds to the symptoms described above (jaundice, abdominal pain, weakness, swelling, weight loss and/or fever). The liver function tests in HCC often reflect infiltrative malignancy and parenchymal disease, with elevated ALT/AST reflecting hepatocellular injury and elevated ALP/GGT reflecting biliary obstruction from tumour invasion. Further workup would include AFP (blood test) and further imaging (MRCP, ERCP, USS etc).

Pancreatic carcinoma classically presents with painless jaundice and often weight loss. In this case with the abdominal pain and hepatitis B infection HCC is a more likely diagnosis based on epidemiology. Furthermore, the largest risk factors for pancreatic cancer include smoking, chronic pancreatitis, diabetes, older age and male gender. She does not have any of these risk factors described in the stem.

Cholangiocarcinoma is a form of cancer that affects the bile ducts but is rare comprising of 3% of all gastrointestinal tumours. Her presentation would fit with a diagnosis of this, but epidemiologically and with her history of hepatitis B, HCC is more likely. Additional tumour marker testing such as CEA and CA 19-9 plus imaging would help to differentiate these.

Again, fulminant hepatitis is a severe, rapid, and rare condition. It is acute and often rapidly fatal (within days to weeks), hence is unlikely to have the prolonged 6-week history described in this stem. Symptoms rapidly develop with encephalopathy, coagulopathy and other features of liver failure. There is no mention about a deranged mental status or bleeding issues in the stem. The LFTs in fulminant hepatitis would also likely reflect hepatic injury with ALT/AST rise more so than ALP/GGT.

Liver cirrhosis can occur as a complication of hepatitis B infection. The scarring can cause an array of symptoms, including in some cases jaundice, abdominal pain weight loss and fever if there is infection. There may be other features if it is decompensated such as ascites and encephalopathy. The LFTs in cirrhosis can vary, but in late stage can often be normal/mildly raised, but cirrhosis is unlikely to cause the severely deranged LFT picture seen in this case. Cirrhosis is a slow and progressive condition that may not present as acutely unless decompensation occurs. A further work-up with ultrasound/fibroscan would help to determine the underlying problem.
learningPoints: A large risk factor for the development of hepatocellular carcinoma is hepatitis infection (B or C)
Anyone with hepatitis B infection should have regular (6 monthly) ultrasound scans for HCC screening
The most common cause of HCC in Australia is chronic hepatitis B or C infection causing cirrhosis
references: Janevska D, Chaloska-Ivanova V, Janevski V. Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Risk Factors, Diagnosis and Treatment. Open Access Maced J Med Sci. 2015;3(4):732-736. doi:10.3889/oamjms.2015.111
Van Beers BE. Diagnosis of cholangiocarcinoma. HPB (Oxford). 2008;10(2):87-93. doi:10.1080/13651820801992716
Kumar, S. Fulminant Hepatitis. 2020. MSD Manual: Professional Version. Access at: https://www.msdmanuals.com/en-au/professional/hepatic-and-biliary-disorders/hepatitis/fulminant-hepatitis

Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd. Hepatocellular carcinoma (Internet). 2021. Access at: https://tgldcdp-tg-org-au.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/viewTopic?topicfile=hepatocellular-carcinoma&guidelineName=Liver%20Disorders&topicNavigation=navigateTopic#toc_d1e47.
